name: M.S. in Information Technology Administration and Security

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This is the most coherent of the informatics M.S. programs: a clearly named IT-administration spine (IT 610 Systems Administration -> IT 635 Database Administration and IT 640 Network Services Administration; IT 620 Wireless Security) is paired with a strong security stack (CS 608/645/647/696, IS 680/681/682 audit and forensics) and a management layer (MGMT 620/630, EM 636, FIN 600), with a CS 505 bridge on-ramp and deepestPrereqChain=3. requirementGroups=0 means the plan itself is not parsed, but the course set clearly aligns with the degree's dual admin/security focus.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the plan of study making the bridge -> core administration -> security -> management progression explicit so the coherent design is visible to students.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The internal graduate chain is clean (deepestPrereqChain=3; IT 610->IT 635, IT 610/CS 645->IT 640, CS 645->CS 647), but missingCourseCount=7 flags dangling references (IS 331, IT 114/120/220/230/340/420) that are undergraduate bridge prerequisites absent from the graduate catalog rather than curricular errors.
dimensions.suggestion: Link the seven bridge prerequisites (IT 114/120/220/230/340/420, IS 331) to the undergraduate catalog so the dangling references resolve and the bridge pathway is explicit.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, so the administration and security competencies the degree certifies are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 outcomes covering systems/network/database administration and security operations, ideally mapped to a workforce framework (NICE).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: For a full M.S., statedTotalCredits is null and requirementGroups=0, so the total (typically ~30 credits) and the split among bridge, core, security, and management courses are not transparent; creditReconcileDelta is null so consistency cannot be checked.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and how it divides among bridge, administration core, security, and management/elective courses.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The named IT and security courses (IT 610/620/635/640, IS 680/681/682) make this page more legible than the sibling IS degrees, and descriptions are specific (IT 640 on web and domain services; IS 682 on computer-forensics evidence handling), but there is still no plan of study, no credit total, and unresolved bridge references.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a labeled plan of study and credit summary, and surface the bridge requirements explicitly on the page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Coverage is current and job-aligned: systems/network/database administration (IT 610/635/640), wireless and 5G security (IT 620, ECE 645), cloud and IoT security (ECE 683), cloud computing (CS 643), counter-hacking (CS 647), forensics (IS 682), and IT service management (IS 678). The gaps are integrated cloud security/DevSecOps and a security-operations (SOC/SIEM) course.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a cloud-security/DevSecOps course and a security-operations (SOC/SIEM/incident-response) elective to match current enterprise IT-security roles.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and role-oriented (IT 610 on host/server administration tasks; IT 620 on wireless LAN vulnerabilities and attacks; ECE 683 on cloud/IoT networking security); none are thin or placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Graduate prerequisites are clear and the internal chain is consistent (IT 610->IT 635/IT 640), but seven dangling references to undergraduate bridge courses (IT 114/120/220/230/340/420, IS 331) and some description text in prereq fields (CS 696) reduce overall clarity.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 643 (cloud) and the security courses exist separately, but no course integrates IAM, cloud misconfiguration, and secure CI/CD for administrators.
IS 682 covers forensics after the fact, but there is no course on detection, monitoring, and live incident response that IT/security teams run daily.
Network and systems admin (IT 610/640) lack a dedicated treatment of IAM and zero-trust architecture now central to enterprise security.
IT 610 teaches traditional host/server admin; Kubernetes/container operations dominant in modern infrastructure are not represented.
IS 680/681 touch auditing, but structured risk management and compliance frameworks (NIST, ISO 27001) are not deepened.

emergingTrends.rationale: Extending IT 640/CS 696 network security toward identity-based, perimeter-less models matches where enterprises are moving.
Building on IT 610 and CS 643, automated, declarative infrastructure is now standard administrator practice.
Pairing CS 647 and IS 682 with ML would enable automated threat detection and triage, a fast-growing SOC capability.
ECE 645 (5G) and ECE 683 (cloud/IoT security) position the program for securing massively connected environments.
Automation, monitoring, and reliability practices extend IT 610/IT 640 administration toward modern operations roles.

topSuggestions: Publish the plan of study and total credit requirement (~30) showing the bridge -> administration core -> security -> management progression.
Resolve the seven dangling bridge references (IT 114/120/220/230/340/420, IS 331) by linking the undergraduate courses.
Add a cloud-security/DevSecOps course and a security-operations (SOC/SIEM/incident-response) elective to close the main currency gaps.
Add IAM/zero-trust and container-administration coverage to modernize the systems/network admin core.
Publish 3-5 program-level learning outcomes for administration and security, mapped to a workforce framework.
